student_name: Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari
hub_subtitle: Inglês do dia a dia — apresentar-se, rotina e conversação

lesson.menu_title: Midpoint Review -- How Far Has Ana Paula Come?
lesson.menu_desc: A midpoint review of Lessons 1-12 -- your English journey so far, mixing the tenses you have learned: present, past, future and modals -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 13 -- Midpoint Review
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari | Lesson 13 -- Midpoint Review
lesson.grammar_point: review of tenses (present, past, future, modals)

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
a long trip, or the process of learning and growing over time
b
the things you usually do every day
c
the area around your home where you live
d
an activity you do for fun in your free time
e
a plan of when things happen during your day or week
f
something you want to achieve in the future
g
an important moment or achievement in your life
h
a chance to do something good
i
in or to a foreign country
j
the best or most memorable part of an experience
k
to succeed in dealing with a problem or difficulty
l
a list of things you want to do in your life

paras: When I started this English course, I could only say a few words, and I felt nervous every time I had to speak. Today, halfway through, I feel like a different person. I have a busy routine: I work, I take care of my family, and I still find time to study almost every day. Learning a new language is a long journey, and I am proud of how far I have come.

In the first lessons, I learned to talk about my neighborhood, my favorite hobby, and my daily schedule. Later, I described my goals for the future and the milestones I want to reach. Every class has been an opportunity to grow. I have made many mistakes, of course, but I have also overcome most of them. Each small success is a real highlight for me.

Now I can hold a real conversation, and I will keep practicing until I feel completely confident. One day, I am going to work abroad as a doctor, and speaking English well is the key to that dream. It sits right at the top of my bucket list. The second half of this course is only beginning, and I am ready for it.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B1 review reading, a learner looks back at her first twelve lessons

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Learning English is too difficult and she wants to stop the course.
Ana Paula looks back at her progress so far and feels proud and ready for the second half.
It is a list of grammar rules you must memorize before the next lesson.

items: At the start of the course, Ana Paula could only say a few words.
The text says: 'When I started this English course, I could only say a few words.'
Ana Paula has a lot of free time and rarely studies.
The text says: 'I have a busy routine ... and I still find time to study almost every day.'
Ana Paula has overcome most of her mistakes.
The text says: 'I have made many mistakes ... but I have also overcome most of them.'
Ana Paula wants to work abroad as a doctor one day.
The text says: 'One day, I am going to work abroad as a doctor.'

gapfill.text: One story, many tenses. When you talk about your progress, you naturally mix tenses: the present for your routine now (I have a busy routine), the past for the beginning (I started, I learned), the present perfect for the whole journey (I have overcome many mistakes), the future for your plans (I am going to work abroad, I will keep practicing), and modals for ability (I can hold a conversation). Choose the tense by the TIME you mean, not by the word.

bank.label: Useful sentences to talk about your progress
items: When I started, I could only say a few words.
I have a busy routine, but I still study every day.
Learning English has been a long journey.
I have already reached a few milestones.
I have overcome many of my mistakes.
Now I can hold a real conversation.
I will keep practicing until I feel confident.
One day, I am going to work abroad.

lf.title: Which time does each sentence talk about?

lf.text: Notice the four times: the past for the beginning (I started, I learned), the present for now (I have, I work), the present perfect for the whole journey so far (I have overcome), and the future for plans (I am going to, I will). Same story, different tenses.

items.situation: Think back to Lesson 1. How did you feel about speaking English then, and how do you feel now?
Describe your daily routine and schedule in three sentences.
What is one milestone you have reached in this course so far?
What is one mistake you have overcome, and how?
tips: I used to say ..., but now I ...

items.situation: What is one goal on your bucket list for the second half of the course?
tips: One day, I am going to ...

items: Tell me about your first lessons. What did you learn, and how did you feel? Use the past simple.
Describe your English routine today: when and how you study. Use the present simple.
Talk about your whole journey so far: the milestones you have reached and the mistakes you have overcome. Use the present perfect.
Look ahead: what are your goals for the second half of the course, and what is on your English bucket list? Use will and going to.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model answer
list: Model: 'When I started, I could only say a few words. Now I have a busy routine, but I still study every day. So far, I have reached a few milestones and overcome many mistakes. One day, I am going to work abroad, and I will keep practicing until I feel confident.'
The beginning: past simple (I started, I learned, I felt).
Right now: present simple (I have, I work, I study).
The whole journey so far: present perfect (I have reached, I have overcome).
Plans and ability: future and modals (I am going to work, I will practice, I can hold a conversation).
answerkey.note: In a review, you naturally mix tenses. Choose each tense by the time you mean: past for the start, present for now, present perfect for the journey so far, future and modals for what comes next.

listenings.text: I have been learning English for about a year now, and I still remember my very first class. When I started, I could barely introduce myself; I knew only a few words and I felt so nervous. My routine was already busy, so finding time to study was hard, but I decided to keep going. Little by little, I learned to talk about my neighborhood, my daily schedule, my hobbies, and my plans for the future. Looking back, the biggest highlight is not one single lesson; it is how much more confident I feel today. I have made a lot of mistakes, but I have overcome most of them, and each mistake taught me something. I am only halfway through my journey, and I already cannot wait for the second half.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sarah! I just finished my midpoint review with my teacher, and it made me think about you. Can you believe how far we have both come? When we started, we could only say very simple sentences, and now we can actually have a real conversation. My teacher asked me about my goals for the second half of the course, and I realized I have so many. I want to travel abroad and use my English on the trip; that is right at the top of my bucket list. I still have things to improve, of course, but every class is a new opportunity. Anyway, tell me: what is the biggest milestone you have reached so far, and what do you want to accomplish next? Call me back, I really want to know. Bye!

extra_audio.text: When I started this course, I could only say a few words. In the first lessons, I learned to describe my neighborhood and my daily routine. Later, I talked about my goals and the milestones I want to reach. Along the way, I have overcome many of my mistakes. Now I am ready for the second half of my English journey.
